sections.title: Green School
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens with Aasha and Mohit discussing what they love about school and introducing the concept of a 'green school' — one that manages waste, saves water, uses electricity carefully, and has a green campus. Aasha shares this idea with classmates and their teacher Anupam, sparking curiosity about how to make their own school a green school. The section establishes school as a safe, caring place for learning and introduces the aspiration to improve it.

tasks.task_text: Walk around your school and create its map. Include the main building, playground, garden, water tanks and other key features. Do not forget the school gate and the pathways that connect the buildings.
What do I really like about my school?
How can I make my school greener?
In case there is any difficulty at school, whom do I talk to or inform?

sections.title: Be a School Explorer!
sections.content_summary: Students form 'School Explorer Teams' with roles such as Water Watchers, Electricity Savers, Waste Warriors, Green Guardians, and Traffic Trackers to observe and report on different aspects of their school environment. Each team is given observation prompts to guide their inquiry — checking for leaking taps, wasted electricity, litter, plants and trees, and traffic issues near the school gate. The section draws on the traditional Indian practice of cleaning floors with cow dung and mud to keep insects away and maintain coolness, situating local ecological knowledge alongside modern environmental observation. After exploring, teams discuss their findings with the class using structured reflection questions.

sections.title: Waste Management
sections.content_summary: This section explains how to reduce waste through reuse and mindful consumption, and how remaining waste (food peels, leftovers, paper) should be segregated into dry and wet categories. Students learn the colour coding of bins (green for wet waste, blue for dry waste) and the role of traditional recyclers who collect specific items such as old newspapers. The section connects to IKS by recognising that traditional recyclers — known by different names in different regions — have long practised community-based material recovery. Students are prompted to find out what recyclers in their own area collect and where the materials go.

tasks.task_text: Can you name the colour of the bin used for the following? Dry waste: _________________ Wet waste: _________________
What things does your school give to recyclers?
Ask your parents and find out if there are any items that they had set aside to give to the recyclers. What are they?
With the help of your teacher, ask a recycler to visit your class and share what they do at work. It will be interesting to find out where these items go and how they can be reused or recycled.

Make small paper chits (30–40) with the names or drawings of the different items shown below. Add more items to the list based on what you see around your school or home. Take three empty boxes, and label them—green for wet waste, blue for dry waste, and the third can be labelled as 'Items for recycler'. One by one, pick a chit and put it in the box that is correct. After all the chits have been put in the boxes, take one box at a time and discuss whether it has been put correctly.
What happens if we do not separate our waste?

What can we do to make the work of sanitation workers easier and safer? Ask your teacher to invite a member of the sanitation workers to your classroom. Ask them to share what they do, the challenges they face, and how we can play a better role in waste management.

sections.title: Let Us be Cool!
sections.content_summary: This section explores why some rooms feel warmer than others, connecting the phenomenon of heat absorption and reflection to practical school design choices. Trees, ventilated windows, and white roofs are identified as natural ways to keep spaces cool, while black surfaces absorb heat. The IKS element here is the marginal note that in many villages, people clean floors with cow dung and mud to keep insects away and keep the house cool — a traditional practice aligned with natural thermal management. Activity 3 has students compare the heat absorbed by black and white painted tiles left in the Sun.

tasks.task_text: Take two tiles or stones, paint one black and the other white. Keep them under the Sun. After a while, touch each of them and feel the difference in their temperature. Which one felt cooler?
If you have to paint the roof of your school or home white, what would happen?
If your area is getting warmer, what actions can be taken to keep it cool?

sections.title: Dripping Water
sections.content_summary: The section focuses on the invisible scale of water wastage from small leaks, prompting students to quantify how much water a dripping tap wastes over a day. It builds on the earlier school explorer observation about leaking taps and extends it to a measurable activity. The IKS note highlights clay pots (matkas) as a traditional, electricity-free cooling technology for drinking water — a smart design rooted in evaporative cooling. The Jal Shakti Abhiyan government campaign is introduced as a civic context for school-based water conservation.

tasks.task_text: Look out for a dripping tap or poke a small hole in a used water bottle filled with water. Now, let the water drip. Collect the dripping water in a glass and carefully measure the time it took for the glass to fill up. Time taken to fill one glass: How many glasses would become full in one day?
What are some easy things we can do in our school to prevent the wastage of water?
How can you collect and use rainwater at home or in school?

sections.title: Greenery Around Us
sections.content_summary: This section highlights the ecological and thermal importance of trees in the school campus — trees provide shade, clean air, food and habitat for birds and insects, and help keep surroundings cool. Students observe and name trees on the campus and identify birds and insects near them. Activity 5 is a controlled temperature experiment comparing water kept under a tree versus under the Sun, demonstrating natural cooling. The IKS note references Ladakh's 'ice stupas' — tall ice towers built by students that melt slowly in summer to water school gardens — illustrating a traditional engineering response to water scarcity.

tasks.task_text: Name the trees on your campus or near your school.
Which birds and insects have you seen near the plants or trees on your school campus?

1. Take two glasses of water. Measure the temperature using a thermometer under the guidance of your teacher. Record your readings. 2. Keep one glass under a tree and the other under the Sun. After one hour, measure the temperature again. Record your readings. What do these readings tell us? 3. With the help of your teacher, try to find out the names of the trees and plants that attract birds, and butterflies. Try to create a butterfly garden in your school.

sections.title: Traffic Signs
sections.content_summary: Building on Thomas's concern about road accidents near school, this section explains why traffic rules exist and introduces the importance of following them. Students research and write traffic rules, observe what happens at the school gate, and work in teams to design a safety signboard for the school gate using words, drawings, or symbols. The section emphasises civic responsibility and student agency in making public spaces safer.

tasks.task_text: Find out and write at least three traffic rules.
Think and write about what you saw at the school gate.
Besides making signboards, what else can we do to improve traffic near the school?

sections.title: Fire Safety
sections.content_summary: This section teaches students how to stay safe during a fire emergency through a structured fire drill practice. Students locate their school's fire extinguisher and assembly point, and learn what to do if they smell smoke. The six-step fire drill procedure covers moving calmly to exits, crawling under smoke, avoiding hiding places, helping others safely, reaching the assembly point, and alerting staff. The section emphasises preparedness and calm action over panic.

tasks.task_text: Where is the fire extinguisher kept in your school?
In case of a fire, where is the assembly point in your school?
What should you do if there is a fire or if you smell smoke?

sections.title: What We do Matters!
sections.content_summary: This section shifts from environmental stewardship to social behaviour, emphasising that how students behave with others at school, home, and public places shapes whether those spaces are welcoming or uncomfortable. Five real-life scenarios of poor behaviour (queue-jumping, vandalism, littering, teasing, noise in public) are presented for group discussion, prompting students to consider what they would do and how to prevent such incidents. The section concludes by affirming that respect, kindness, and polite behaviour are demonstrated through everyday actions.

tasks.task_text: In small groups, discuss the situations given below. Each group will discuss one situation. One student from each group will then share their ideas with the class. Situations: 1. Two students are pushing and skipping the line at the water tap while others are waiting. 2. A student is scribbling on a classroom bench with a marker. Another child watches but says nothing. 3. Someone throws a wrapper in the playground. A younger student copies the same act. 4. One student keeps teasing and making fun of another student. 5. A group of students are making noise in a public park and disturbing a small group doing yoga nearby. Each group would discuss: 1. If you see such a situation unfold before you, what would you do? 2. What steps can we take to prevent such incidents?

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The closing reflection section brings together all the chapter's themes — environmental care, safety, and social behaviour — through nine varied tasks that ask students to think, create, write, act and survey. Students write letters, diary entries, and speeches; draw a happiness tree; role-play situations of kindness; and conduct a peer survey. The section moves students from school-focused action to imagining how the same values of happiness, safety, and care can be extended to homes, neighbourhoods, and communities.

tasks.task_text: Out of all the classrooms in your school, which one do you think is the nicest and why?

Create a happiness tree. On a wall or chart paper, draw a large tree with many branches. Each student should add a 'leaf' with one small action written on it that makes the school a happier place (for example, greeting someone, turning off lights, sharing space, etc.).
Sharing one's feelings is exciting. Write a letter to your teacher on 'My School— My Happy Place'.
Which actions would you extend to your home, neighbourhood and communities to make it a happy place for all? Describe any one of them in detail.

Imagine a day in school from someone else's perspective. Choose any one of the following: A student who navigates school using a wheelchair. A student who speaks a different mother tongue. A sanitation worker at school. Write a diary entry from their point of view. What they felt? What was easy or hard? What made them smile or worry?
As principal for a day, what three changes would you make to enhance happiness, safety and greenery in your school?

In groups of 4–5, act out the following: A student showing kindness in a difficult situation. A team solving a school water wastage problem. A student helping a shy classmate feel included. After each role-play, ask the class: What did you see? What was inspiring? Could this happen in real-life?
Write a one minute speech for your morning assembly on 'My Dream School'. Mention what makes it special, who helps it stay that way and what every student can do.

Take a quick survey, ask five students and one teacher: (a) What is the one thing that makes them feel happy in school? (b) What is the one thing that could be improved? (c) What is the one kind thing that they saw today? Record and present your findings to the class. What did you learn from others?

dual_strand.natural: Environmental systems of the school campus — heat, water, waste, and greenery as interconnected natural processes
dual_strand.human_cultural: Civic responsibility and community stewardship — managing school and public spaces through waste segregation, water conservation, green practices, traffic safety, and respectful behaviour
